content:
5 min read
Just now

--

You added async def to your route because that's what the FastAPI docs do. Your endpoint works. Requests come back with 200s. Everything looks fine in dev with two users hitting it.
Then you ship it, traffic goes up, and your “async” app starts responding slower than the sync version you replaced. No errors. No stack traces. Just a service that quietly stops scaling the way you promised your team it would.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I’ve shipped this exact bug into production. Here are the seven mistakes behind it — and the fix for each one.
1. Calling blocking code inside an async function
This is the one that gets almost everyone. Marking a function async doesn't make the code inside it non-blocking. If you call a synchronous library function, it still blocks the event loop — for everyone, not just the current request.
# Before: looks async, behaves sync
@app.get("/users/{user_id}")
async def get_user(user_id: int):
response = requests.get(f"https://api.example.com/users/{user_id}")
return response.json()requests is synchronous. While that call runs, your entire event loop is frozen — no other request on that worker can make progress, even ones that have nothing to do with this endpoint.
